questions.prompt: Why use `local` for variables inside bash functions?

choices.text: Without `local`, variables defined inside a function are global by default — they persist after the function returns and can clobber caller variables with the same name. Example: function uses `i=0` as a loop counter; caller had its own `i` for something else; calling the function corrupts the caller's `i`. With `local i=0`, the function's `i` is scoped to that function only. Standard hygiene: `local` every variable you declare inside a function unless you specifically want it to leak. The same rule applies to function arguments stored in named variables: `local name="$1"`. Bash globals are a footgun the way globals were before any modern language; `local` is the antidote. Discipline here prevents the worst class of script bugs.

questions.explanation: Without local, function vars leak globally and can clobber caller's state. Always use local.

questions.prompt: You want to read each line of a file into an array. Best way?

choices.text: `mapfile -t lines < file` (also called `readarray`). Reads each line into the array `lines`; `-t` strips the trailing newline from each element. Now you can iterate `for line in "${lines[@]}"; do ... done` cleanly. Alternative `cat file | while read line; do ... done` has two problems: (1) it spawns a subshell for the while loop, so variables set inside don't persist to the outer script — a frequent footgun, (2) it processes line-by-line without storing them, so you can't iterate twice. `mapfile` is the modern idiomatic approach, available since bash 4. For very large files where you don't want everything in memory, the `while read` pattern (with proper `< file` redirection, NOT `cat | while`) is fine: `while IFS= read -r line; do ...; done < file`.

questions.explanation: `mapfile -t lines < file` reads into array cleanly. Avoids subshell trap of `cat | while`.
